nativeRange.habitat: Coral reefs and rubble zones below the intertidal, where the shrimp typically hides in crevices or beneath rocks during the day and emerges to hunt sea stars at night.

decorPreferences: mature live rock with crevices and overhangs

diet.notes: The Harlequin Shrimp is an obligate predator of echinoderms, feeding almost exclusively on live sea stars. Starfish tube feet and soft tissue make up the primary food source, and the species has been documented occasionally taking live sea urchins when sea stars are unavailable. It generally cannot be weaned onto prepared, frozen, or non-echinoderm foods, so a sustainable supply of live sea stars is required for long-term care. Commonly used prey species include chocolate-chip stars, Linckia, and pest Asterina stars harvested from other reef tanks. A single 4–6" sea star can reportedly sustain a pair for several weeks, as the shrimp consume the animal slowly while it continues to regenerate.

goodWith: other peaceful marine shrimp (when given separate territory)
avoidWith: all desirable sea stars (Linckia, Fromia, sand-sifting stars, brittle stars)
aggressive or shrimp-eating fish (large wrasses, triggerfish, hawkfish, lionfish, eels)
same-sex conspecifics outside a bonded pair
other Harlequin Shrimp pairs in the same tank

summary: The Harlequin Shrimp is a vividly patterned marine shrimp from Indo-Pacific coral reefs, recognized by its white body marked with bold spots of blue-edged purple or red. It feeds almost exclusively on live sea stars and is generally kept as a bonded male-female pair in a dedicated or carefully managed reef setup.
careNotes: The Harlequin Shrimp is a highly specialist species whose entire husbandry revolves around providing live sea stars as food. It is generally considered reef-safe with corals and most non-echinoderm tankmates, but it will systematically eliminate any sea stars in the aquarium — including desirable ornamental species such as Linckia, Fromia, and sand-sifting stars, as well as brittle stars. A Harlequin Shrimp should not be added to any setup that contains sea stars the keeper wants to preserve. Because feeding requires a steady supply of live echinoderms, many keepers maintain a dedicated nano or small reef tank for the shrimp and cycle in pest Asterina stars harvested from other aquariums, supplemented by purchased chocolate-chip stars (Protoreaster nodosus) when available. A bonded pair will typically work together to flip a sea star and begin consuming its tube feet while the star is still alive; this is normal feeding behavior but can be distressing to observe. The Harlequin Shrimp is best kept as a bonded male-female pair — multiple same-sex individuals or unbonded pairs have been reported to fight, sometimes fatally. The older scientific name Hymenocera elegans is still encountered in hobby sources and trade listings, and the species was long placed in family Gnathophyllidae; current taxonomy places it in Palaemonidae. The species is highly sensitive to copper and must never be exposed to copper-based medications. Adequate iodine and trace-element supplementation is commonly advised to support healthy molting. Because of the diet specialization and the practical difficulty of maintaining a live-prey supply alongside a broader reef community, a species-focused or species-only tank setup is widely recommended.
breedingNotes: The Harlequin Shrimp has been bred in captivity and is generally considered moderately difficult to raise through to settlement. Bonded pairs form lasting attachments and will reproduce repeatedly when water conditions remain stable. Females carry the eggs attached beneath the abdomen for approximately two to three weeks before hatching; clutch size reportedly ranges from around 100 to several thousand eggs depending on the size and condition of the female. After hatching, the larvae enter a free-swimming planktonic phase that has been reported to last five to six weeks or longer. During this phase the larvae are very difficult to feed in a standard home aquarium, and survival to settlement is generally low without dedicated larval-rearing infrastructure.

primary.name: Aqua Imports
primary.notes: Primary source — confirmed scientific name Hymenocera picta, origin Indonesia, ~2" adult size, temperature 74-78°F, pH 8.2-8.4, salinity 35 ppt (SG ≈ 1.026), obligate echinoderm diet, reef-safe.

additional.name: LiveAquaria (Blue Zoo Aquatics page used as fallback)
additional.notes: Designated LiveAquaria product URL returned HTTP 404; LiveAquaria search confirmed the product exists at /products/harlequin-shrimp but full care details were not retrievable through WebFetch. Substituted BlueZoo's H. elegans page (modern synonym of H. picta) for the second cross-source. Cross-confirmed: family Gnathophyllidae (older placement), adult size ~1.5", temperature 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.023-1.025, dKH 8-12, carnivore, peaceful, reef-compatible, copper-sensitive, requires iodine for molting.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Hymenocera picta)
additional.notes: Taxonomy authority — modern family placement Palaemonidae, order Decapoda, H. elegans as junior synonym, ~5 cm adult length, Indo-Pacific + Hawaiian distribution, obligate starfish diet, pair-living behavior, female fecundity 100-5,000 eggs.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed 50 L minimum (reference for the cautious 75 L pick), 5 cm max length, Central/West/East Pacific range, typical reef temperature ~26°C and SG ~1.026.

additional.name: Aquarium Source care guide
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed temperature 72-82°F, pH 8.0-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.023-1.025, lifespan up to ~7 years, 10-gal minimum for a single shrimp / 20-gal for a pair, breeding behavior (2-3 week egg carry, 5-6 week planktonic larval phase), male-male fatal aggression.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Molts periodically as it grows, with adequate iodine and trace-element levels widely considered important for successful exuviation. Discarded exoskeletons are typically left undisturbed in the tank and may be re-consumed for calcium. Specific intermolt-period data for H. picta is not well documented in hobby sources.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Bonded pairs spawn readily in stable water conditions, but the primary challenge lies in rearing the larvae. The planktonic larval phase is long-lived and requires specialized larval-culture equipment and feeding regimes that are not typical of a home reef aquarium.
